planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after cold stratification, or cold-stratify seeds 60 days and sow indoors in late winter; Short's aster prefers well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun at woodland edges and open forest clearings.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun at woodland edges and open woodland clearings; Short's aster is a native aster of the central and upper South, producing a generous display of lavender-blue to violet daisy flowers in late summer and fall that are highly attractive to monarch butterflies, painted ladies, and numerous native bees. The heart-shaped lower leaves and clasping upper leaves distinguish it from many similar asters. It is naturally found at the margins between forest and open areas — edges, clearings, and open woods — making it well adapted to the semi-shaded conditions of the typical home landscape under trees. Named for Dr. Charles Short, a 19th-century Kentucky botanist. Plant in masses for the best butterfly-attracting effect in late summer garden borders.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; white boneset grows naturally in dry to moist, sandy, acidic, open pine woods, flatwoods, and sandhills of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain. It is better adapted to dry, sandy conditions than common boneset.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, sandy, lean, acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; white boneset is a Coastal Plain native eupatorium of dry sandy pinewoods and sandhills, particularly common across the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain from Virginia to Texas. Unlike the moisture-loving common boneset (E. perfoliatum), white boneset thrives in dry, sandy, acid soils of longleaf pine flatwoods — making it an outstanding native pollinator plant for dry, infertile, sandy soils where few other flowering perennials excel. The flat-topped clusters of pure white flowers in late summer attract an extraordinary diversity of small native bees, wasps, flies, beetles, and butterflies including both skippers and swallowtails. Plant in dry, sandy naturalistic garden settings in zone 9a where it requires almost no supplemental water once established.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination after natural cold stratification; whorled rosinweed prefers well-drained to dry, moderately fertile to lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade. Slow to establish from seed but long-lived and deep-rooted once mature.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; whorled rosinweed is a distinctive Silphium species recognized by its leaves arranged in neat whorls of 3–4 around the stem — an unusual arrangement among the rosinweeds, most of which have opposite or alternate leaves. The bright yellow daisy flowers in mid to late summer attract native bees and provide seed food for goldfinches. Like all Silphiums, it develops a deep taproot over years and becomes extremely drought tolerant once fully established, but is slow to transplant and performs best when seeded in place. The genus name Silphium references the mysterious ancient plant traded from North Africa in classical times, though the American rosinweeds are unrelated to it. An underused native perennial for dry, prairie-style gardens and naturalistic meadow plantings.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring as shoots emerge; tokudama hosta is slow-growing but extremely long-lived once established. Plant in humus-rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Takes 3–5 years to reach mature size — plan to leave it undisturbed long-term.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; tokudama hosta is one of the most prized species hostas in cultivation, valued for its exceptionally deeply cupped, puckered, glaucous-blue to blue-gray leaves that are among the most intensely textured of all hostas. The deep cupping and puckering of the heavy, corrugated leaf surface gives an almost sculptural quality that makes each plant a focal point in the shade garden. It is a slow-growing hosta species that takes several years to reach full size, but its garden longevity and unique foliage quality make it worth the wait. Several important hosta cultivars derive from this species, including the famous 'Halcyon' (via hybridization). Pale lavender flowers in mid-summer. Slug damage is less severe on the heavily puckered, waxy leaves than on smooth-leaved hostas.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall immediately after ripening; seeds have a complex dormancy requiring warm + cold stratification and may take 2 years to germinate. Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in woodland settings.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; leatherwood is one of the most unusual and charming native shrubs of eastern North America — a slow-growing, long-lived understory shrub of rich, moist, shaded woodland slopes, particularly common in beech-maple and hemlock forests. The plant blooms very early in spring, before leaf-out, when its clusters of small, pale yellow, tubular flowers appear directly on the bare stems — one of the first native plants to flower in the eastern woodland. The flexible, rubbery stems and bark are extraordinarily tough and difficult to break — early settlers called it 'rope-bark' and used the bark for lashing and binding. The small, yellowish-green berries are consumed by birds. A wonderful, slow-growing native for the understory shade garden in cool climates, zones 3–7.
